nodes.label: Hexagonal modular architecture
nodes.rationale: Use-case modules depend on internal ports so provider SDKs, CLIs, tunnels, and transports remain replaceable.

nodes.label: Backend ↔ Local Agent boundary
nodes.rationale: Every local side effect must go through explicit commands and persisted events.

nodes.label: Agent harness
nodes.rationale: A common harness interface avoids coupling use cases to a single CLI.

nodes.label: PersistenceModule
nodes.rationale: Prisma types stay out of controllers and public API contracts; use cases depend on repository ports.

nodes.label: ToolReadinessModule
nodes.rationale: Developer readiness blocks broken sessions, while PM readiness deliberately excludes local tools.

nodes.label: Session start policy
nodes.rationale: PM-started sessions require project membership, an online owning agent, passing developer readiness, and project policy permission.

nodes.label: Session state machine
nodes.rationale: Explicit transitions make orchestration predictable and invalid event ordering rejectable.

nodes.label: Responsive preview presets
nodes.rationale: Viewport changes are client-side only and require no backend mutation.

nodes.label: Validation before review request
nodes.rationale: A draft review request is allowed only after build, tests, lint, and preview pass in AWAITING_PM_VALIDATION.

nodes.label: Review request creation workflow
nodes.rationale: The agent owns local Git operations while the backend keeps the controlled and auditable source-control identity.

nodes.label: Security and permissions
nodes.rationale: PMs never receive shell access or local secrets; commands are protocol-limited and logs/diffs are redacted.

nodes.label: Prisma persistence boundary
nodes.rationale: Prisma Client is isolated behind repository adapters, and atomic multi-entity changes use transactions.

nodes.label: GitHub Actions CI
nodes.rationale: Repository CI is the quality gate for the PairDock codebase, not a PairDock product feature.

nodes.label: Tailwind CSS and shadcn/ui styling foundation
nodes.rationale: A single app-owned styling/component foundation avoids competing frameworks and premature abstractions.

nodes.label: BT-004 — PM member access is allowed
BT-008 — Backend close session is idempotent
BT-010 — Invalid agent event is rejected
BT-011 — Agent event is streamed to authorized UI
BT-016 — Preview ready is emitted
nodes.rationale: PM must see the rendered app.

nodes.label: BT-018 — Agent prompt runs inside worktree
BT-021 — Sensitive file masked from diff
BT-023 — All checks OK allow review request validation
BT-024 — Failed check blocks review request
BT-028 — Developer creates session with model
BT-030 — Review request blocked without OK validation
BT-031 — Branch pushed before review request
nodes.rationale: Review request must point to a remote branch.

nodes.label: BT-032 — Draft review request created through source-control adapter
BT-034 — Two-column login
nodes.rationale: Clarify the two product entry paths.

nodes.label: BT-050 — Same-email cross-role accounts remain independent
nodes.rationale: Allow one person to test both V1 roles without role/session overwrite.

nodes.label: BT-036 — Use cases decoupled from external providers
nodes.rationale: Providers and harnesses can change without rewriting core business logic.

nodes.label: BT-037 — Prisma Client stays behind persistence adapters
BT-038 — Prisma transaction persists session state atomically
nodes.rationale: Prevent session-state drift after partial writes.

nodes.label: BT-039 — Tailwind CSS and shadcn/ui are the web styling foundation
BT-040 — Prisma migration workflow uses Bun scripts
nodes.rationale: Keep migrations explicit, reproducible, and aligned with Bun.

nodes.label: BT-041 — Persistence adapters stay split by repository responsibility
nodes.rationale: Preserve SRP and prevent a monolithic persistence adapter.

nodes.label: BT-042 — Pull request CI runs repository quality gates
BT-043 — CI uses Bun lockfile and avoids npm lockfiles
nodes.rationale: Keep local and CI tooling consistent.

nodes.label: BT-044 — Developer readiness blocks session start
nodes.rationale: Fail before creating a broken worktree/session.

nodes.label: BT-045 — PM does not need local tool readiness
BT-046 — PM can start a session for a shared ready project
nodes.rationale: Match the PM-started shared-project flow without terminal access.

nodes.label: BT-047 — PM cannot start unavailable or unshared projects
nodes.rationale: Prevent unauthorized access and broken local-agent sessions.

nodes.label: BT-048 — PM dashboard reflects start availability
BT-049 — Developer shares a project with a PM
nodes.rationale: Shared-project access is distinct from per-session membership.
